mnemonics.title: Memory Allocation Methods
mnemonics.content: FIBW - First Fit, Ideal Best, Worst Worst - helps remember First Fit, Best Fit, Worst Fit allocation strategies in order of speed and efficiency trade-offs.

mnemonics.title: Fragmentation Types
mnemonics.content: Internal = INside (wasted inside allocated block), External = EXternal (wasted outside/external to allocated blocks).

mnemonics.title: Paging Components
mnemonics.content: Pages go with Frames - Pages (logical) map to Frames (physical). Think: Pages = Book chapters, Frames = physical shelf slots.

mnemonics.title: Segmentation vs Paging
mnemonics.content: Segmentation = Sliced like a sandwich (variable sizes based on type), Paging = Pages in a book (fixed sizes).

acronyms.usage: Hardware component that translates virtual addresses to physical addresses in paging systems.
High-speed cache that stores recent page table entries for faster address translation.
Software that manages memory allocation, process scheduling, and hardware resources.
Primary memory in a computer system where data and programs are stored during execution.
Page replacement algorithm that evicts the oldest page from memory.
Page replacement algorithm that evicts the least recently used page.

visualTips.visualization: Draw memory as a long bar. For contiguous: draw one process as a solid block. For non-contiguous (paging): draw process split into equal-sized chunks scattered across memory.
Draw a partition as a rectangle. Internal: show extra space inside the rectangle. External: show multiple rectangles with gaps (holes) between them.
Draw two boxes - left (virtual page numbers 0,1,2...) and right (physical frame numbers). Draw arrows showing mapping from left to right boxes.
Draw one large square. Split into two equal smaller squares (buddies). Continue splitting until the smallest needed size. Show how they can recombine.

analogies.analogy: Think of memory like a parking lot with parking spaces. Fixed partitioning is like having only large truck spots. Variable partitioning is like having differently sized spots for different vehicles.
Like a library book delivery system: you request chapters (pages) from different parts of a book, and the library sends just those specific pages (frames) to you, not the whole book.
Like using a cloud storage service: you have access to more files (memory) than can fit on your local computer (RAM) by using remote storage (disk).
Like a parking lot with cars parked in different spots leaving small gaps. Even though total empty space exists, no single gap is big enough for a large vehicle.
Like a book's index: instead of searching every page for a topic, you use the index (TLB) to quickly find where information is located.
